ethical_notes: Destroyed by Hernán Cortés and indigenous allies during the siege of 1521. The Templo Mayor (Huēyi Teōcalli) was deliberately demolished stone by stone and the Catedral Metropolitana was built directly on its sacred precinct as a calculated act of religious and cultural erasure. The population collapsed from an estimated 200,000-300,000 to a few thousand within a generation due to the conquest, smallpox and subsequent epidemics (huey cocoliztli). The Mexica chronicles (Codex Aubin, Codex Mendoza, Florentine Codex) preserve parts of the record despite Spanish censorship. Mexica texts and oral traditions continue to be suppressed in Mexican curricula in favor of a homogenized mestizo narrative (ETHICS-009, ETHICS-001).

sources.citation: Matos Moctezuma, E. (1988). The Great Temple of the Aztecs: Treasures of Tenochtitlan. Thames & Hudson.
Smith, M. E. (2012). The Aztecs (3rd ed.). Wiley-Blackwell.
León-Portilla, M. (1992). The Broken Spears: The Aztec Account of the Conquest of Mexico. Beacon Press.

name_variants.name: México-Tenochtitlan
name_variants.context: Hispanized form used during early colonial period

name_variants.name: Ciudad de México
name_variants.context: Name imposed after Spanish conquest; Templo Mayor was deliberately demolished and the Catedral Metropolitana was built on its foundation

name_variants.name: Mexico City
name_variants.context: English exonym used after Mexican independence

ethical_notes: Inca imperial capital conquered by Francisco Pizarro in 1533. The Qorikancha (Sun Temple) was dismantled by the Spanish and the Iglesia de Santo Domingo was built on its walls — another explicit case of architectural superimposition as an instrument of religious conquest. Hispanized spelling 'Cusco' was retained until the municipal council officially restored 'Cuzco' and more recently the Quechua-influenced 'Qosqo' in bilingual contexts. Indigenous Quechua speakers were systematically marginalized for centuries; the language only received co-official status in 1975 (ETHICS-009, ETHICS-001).

sources.citation: D'Altroy, T. N. (2014). The Incas (2nd ed.). Wiley-Blackwell.
MacCormack, S. (1991). Religion in the Andes: Vision and Imagination in Early Colonial Peru. Princeton University Press.

name_variants.name: Cuzco
name_variants.context: Hispanized form imposed after Spanish conquest

name_variants.name: Cusco
name_variants.context: Modern official Spanish spelling

name_variants.name: Qosqo
name_variants.context: Modern Quechua-restored form in bilingual signage

ethical_notes: The name 'Teōtihuācān' ('place where gods were made') is the Nahuatl name given by the later Aztecs who found the city already in ruins — the actual founders and their self-designation are unknown. The ethnic identity of the builders (Totonac, Otomí, Nahua, a multi-ethnic polity, or something else) remains academically contested. Attributing the site categorically to any one modern indigenous group risks ideological misuse. ETHICS-010 applies: the primary original name cannot be determined and the attested Nahuatl name is documented transparently as an attribution by a successor culture.

sources.citation: Cowgill, G. L. (2015). Ancient Teotihuacan: Early Urbanism in Central Mexico. Cambridge University Press.
Manzanilla, L. R. (2017). Teotihuacan: City of Water, City of Fire. University of California Press.

name_variants.name: Teotihuacán
name_variants.context: Hispanized spelling

ethical_notes: Aymara heartland. Colonial Spanish sources (Cieza de León) recorded the name as 'Tiahuanaco' — the modern Aymara-orthography 'Tiwanaku' has been restored in Bolivian policy since 2009 as part of the Plurinational State's recognition of indigenous nations. The site was looted for building stone throughout the colonial and republican periods; portions of the monumental complex were dynamited in the 19th century for railway ballast. This destruction was framed as 'progress' — a pattern repeated across indigenous South American sites.

sources.citation: Janusek, J. W. (2008). Ancient Tiwanaku. Cambridge University Press.
Kolata, A. L. (1993). The Tiwanaku: Portrait of an Andean Civilization. Blackwell.

name_variants.name: Tiahuanaco
name_variants.context: Hispanized colonial spelling

ethical_notes: Pre-Inca Andean ceremonial center. The original Chavín-horizon language and self-designation are unknown; the modern name combines a local Quechua term with the Spanish 'de Huántar'. The Lanzón monolith remains in situ, but many associated artifacts were removed to Lima and foreign museums in the late 19th and 20th centuries without community consent.

sources.citation: Burger, R. L. (1992). Chavin and the Origins of Andean Civilization. Thames & Hudson.
Rick, J. W. (2005). The Evolution of Authority and Power at Chavín de Huántar, Peru. Archeological Papers of the American Anthropological Association, 14(1), 71-89.

ethical_notes: Epigraphic decipherment (Stuart, Houston) reads the emblem glyph as 'Yax Mutal' / 'Mutal'. The modern name 'Tikal' is Yucatec Maya ('at the waterhole') applied centuries after the Classic-period collapse — it is not the original self-designation. The site was 'discovered' by Westerners in the 1840s (Modesto Méndez, Ambrosio Tut) but was well known to local Itza and Mopan Maya communities; the framing of 'discovery' perpetuates colonial erasure of indigenous geographic knowledge.

sources.citation: Martin, S., & Grube, N. (2008). Chronicle of the Maya Kings and Queens (2nd ed.). Thames & Hudson.
Harrison, P. D. (1999). The Lords of Tikal: Rulers of an Ancient Maya City. Thames & Hudson.

name_variants.name: Tikal
name_variants.context: Later Yucatec Maya name meaning 'at the waterhole' adopted after Classic-period abandonment

ethical_notes: 'Lakam Ha' ('Big Water') is the attested Classic-period self-designation decoded from hieroglyphic texts; the polity was called 'Bàakʼ'. The name 'Palenque' is a Spanish colonial word meaning 'palisade/stockade' with no connection to the original. The tomb of K'inich Janaab Pakal was opened by Alberto Ruz Lhuillier in 1952 — a landmark of Mexican archaeology but conducted without consultation of surviving Chʼol Maya descendant communities, as was standard practice of the era.

sources.citation: Martin, S., & Grube, N. (2008). Chronicle of the Maya Kings and Queens (2nd ed.). Thames & Hudson.
Stuart, D., & Stuart, G. (2008). Palenque: Eternal City of the Maya. Thames & Hudson.

name_variants.name: Palenque
name_variants.context: Spanish colonial name meaning 'palisade', unrelated to the original Maya name

ethical_notes: Yucatec Maya name meaning 'at the mouth of the well of the Itza'. Edward H. Thompson purchased the site in 1894 and dredged the Sacred Cenote 1904-1910, shipping thousands of artifacts to the Peabody Museum at Harvard without legitimate Mexican authorization. Partial repatriations have occurred but most objects remain in US collections — a paradigmatic case of extractive archaeology that Mexican authorities cite in ongoing repatriation claims.

sources.citation: Cobos, R. (2004). Chichén Itzá: Settlement and Hegemony during the Terminal Classic Period. In The Terminal Classic in the Maya Lowlands (pp. 517-544). University Press of Colorado.
Coggins, C. C. (Ed.). (1992). Artifacts from the Cenote of Sacrifice, Chichén Itzá, Yucatán. Peabody Museum Press.

name_variants.name: Chichén Itzá
name_variants.context: Hispanized spelling with Spanish diacritics

ethical_notes: One of the oldest urban centers in the Americas (Norte Chico / Caral-Supe civilization, ca. 3000-1800 BCE). The original language is unknown — the modern name derives from the nearby Quechua-speaking village of Caral, whose own naming postdates the abandonment by millennia. Ruth Shady Solís led the research recovery from 1994 and has publicly resisted attempts by external authorities to minimize Andean indigenous authorship of the complex.

sources.citation: Shady Solís, R. (2006). Caral-Supe and the Norte Chico: Complex Society in Peru. In Andean Archaeology III (pp. 28-66). Springer.
Haas, J., Creamer, W., & Ruiz, A. (2004). Dating the late archaic occupation of the Norte Chico region in Peru. Nature, 432(7020), 1020-1023.

ethical_notes: The name 'Cahokia' comes from the unrelated Illiniwek subgroup that occupied the area at early French contact in the late 17th century — they were not descendants of the Mississippian city's builders, whose language and self-name are lost. 19th- and early 20th-century US authors repeatedly attributed the mounds to a 'vanished race' (the 'Moundbuilder myth') rather than to ancestors of living Native American nations, in order to legitimize dispossession of tribes in the Mississippi Valley. Earthen mounds were leveled by farming, railroad construction and suburban development in the 19th and 20th centuries (Big Mound of St. Louis destroyed 1869).

sources.citation: Pauketat, T. R. (2009). Cahokia: Ancient America's Great City on the Mississippi. Viking.
Emerson, T. E., & Lewis, R. B. (Eds.). (2000). Cahokia and the Hinterlands: Middle Mississippian Cultures of the Midwest. University of Illinois Press.

ethical_notes: Ancestral Puebloan (often 'Anasatri' in colonial archaeology — a Navajo-origin term 'Anasází' meaning 'ancient enemies' that descendant Pueblo nations reject and that current scholarship avoids). The term preferred by contemporary descendants is 'Ancestral Puebloan' or specific nation names (Hopi 'Hisatsinom', Zuni, Keresan). The modern name 'Pueblo Bonito' is Spanish-colonial. Richard Wetherill and George Pepper excavated 1896-1900 with Hyde Expedition funds; thousands of artifacts went to the American Museum of Natural History. NAGPRA-era repatriations to descendant Pueblo nations have been partial and contested.

sources.citation: Lekson, S. H. (2018). A Study of Southwestern Archaeology. University of Utah Press.
Plog, S. (2008). Ancient Peoples of the American Southwest (2nd ed.). Thames & Hudson.

ethical_notes: Royal Inca estate of Pachacuti. Never found by the Spanish conquistadors — but 'discovered' is a colonial frame. Local Quechua farmers (including the Richarte and Alvarez families who were cultivating terraces at the site) knew of and lived around the ruins; they informed Hiram Bingham in 1911. Bingham shipped ca. 5000 artifacts to Yale University; return negotiations spanned nearly a century and only began to yield results with the 2010-2012 Yale-Peru agreement. The global framing of Bingham as the 'discoverer' remains a case study in the erasure of indigenous geographic and historical knowledge (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Burger, R. L., & Salazar, L. C. (Eds.). (2004). Machu Picchu: Unveiling the Mystery of the Incas. Yale University Press.
Mould de Pease, M. (2003). Machu Picchu y el código de ética de la Sociedad de Antropología Americana. Institut français d'études andines.

ethical_notes: Capital of the Kingdom of Zimbabwe. The Shona name 'Dzimba-dza-mabwe' ('houses of stone') is the original. From 1871 (Carl Mauch) through the Rhodesian era, colonial-era European authors (Theodore Bent, Richard Hall, and the Rhodes-funded British South Africa Company) systematically denied Shona / African authorship, attributing the complex to Phoenicians, Sabaeans, the Queen of Sheba, or lost 'civilized' Mediterranean peoples. This academic racism was explicit state policy under Rhodesia: the Smith regime pressured archaeologists (including Peter Garlake, who was expelled in 1970) to suppress findings confirming indigenous Shona origin. Attribution to the Shona was only officially embraced after Zimbabwean independence in 1980. The soapstone 'Zimbabwe Birds' were looted by Cecil Rhodes and other Europeans; four of the original eight are now in Zimbabwean museums after long repatriation struggles (one bird returned from Germany in 2003) (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Garlake, P. S. (1973). Great Zimbabwe. Thames & Hudson.
Pikirayi, I. (2001). The Zimbabwe Culture: Origins and Decline of Southern Zambezian States. AltaMira Press.
Fontein, J. (2006). The Silence of Great Zimbabwe: Contested Landscapes and the Power of Heritage. UCL Press.

name_variants.name: Great Zimbabwe
name_variants.context: English exonym used internationally

ethical_notes: Swahili city-state, pre-eminent Indian Ocean trade hub in gold, ivory and enslaved people between the 13th and 15th centuries. Sacked in 1505 by Francisco de Almeida as part of the Portuguese campaign to seize control of Indian Ocean commerce; Fort Gereza was built on top of Kilwa's palace complex. The attack marked the beginning of the forcible reorientation of Swahili coast trade toward Europe. Portuguese chroniclers (Duarte Barbosa, João de Barros) produced accounts that long framed Swahili civilization as 'Arab' or 'Persian' rather than African — a colonial narrative only dismantled by African archaeologists (Chami, Horton) from the 1980s onward (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Chittick, H. N. (1974). Kilwa: An Islamic Trading City on the East African Coast. British Institute in Eastern Africa.
Horton, M., & Middleton, J. (2000). The Swahili: The Social Landscape of a Mercantile Society. Blackwell.
Pouwels, R. L. (2002). East African Coastal History. Journal of African History, 40(3), 385-425.

name_variants.name: Quíloa
name_variants.context: Portuguese colonial form

ethical_notes: Swahili port, historic outlet for the gold of the Zimbabwe plateau. Seized by Pedro de Anaia for Portugal in 1505; Fort São Caetano built the same year. Portuguese sources used 'Sofala' as a blanket label for the entire gold-producing hinterland, erasing distinctions among Shona, Tonga and Swahili polities — a pattern of African geographic flattening visible on most European maps into the 19th century. The original harbor silted up; the modern town of Nova Sofala occupies a shifted site.

sources.citation: Newitt, M. (1995). A History of Mozambique. Indiana University Press.
Sinclair, P. J. J. (1987). Space, Time and Social Formation: A Territorial Approach to the Archaeology and Anthropology of Zimbabwe and Mozambique c. 0-1700 AD. Uppsala University.

name_variants.name: Çofala
name_variants.context: Portuguese colonial spelling

ethical_notes: Swahili port city, the name 'Mvita' in Swahili means 'isle of war' — a reference to its long history of repelling foreign attacks, first by the Portuguese (multiple sieges 1505, 1528, 1589) and later the Omani. The Portuguese Fort Jesus (1593) was built on top of earlier Swahili structures. Under British colonial rule (1895-1963) the city's Swahili elite was displaced by settler and South Asian commercial interests.

sources.citation: Berg, F. J. (1968). The Swahili Community of Mombasa, 1500-1900. Journal of African History, 9(1), 35-56.
Pouwels, R. L. (1987). Horn and Crescent: Cultural Change and Traditional Islam on the East African Coast, 800-1900. Cambridge University Press.

name_variants.name: Mombaça
name_variants.context: Portuguese colonial form

name_variants.name: Mombasa
name_variants.context: British colonial and current English form

ethical_notes: Abandoned Swahili stone-town. The modern spelling 'Gedi' is British colonial. The reasons for abandonment in the 17th century are debated: combinations of Oromo migration pressure, Portuguese disruption of trade, and possibly drops in the water table. Early 20th-century British antiquarians characterized the site as 'mysterious' and implied non-African authorship — a trope paralleling Great Zimbabwe that was dismantled by Kirkman's archaeological work (1948-1958) which confirmed Swahili origin.

sources.citation: Kirkman, J. S. (1954). The Arab City of Gedi: Excavations at the Great Mosque. Oxford University Press.
Pradines, S. (2010). Gedi, une cité portuaire swahilie. Islam médiéval en Afrique orientale. IFAO.

name_variants.name: Gedi
name_variants.context: British colonial spelling

ethical_notes: Capital of the Kingdom of Aksum, one of the earliest Christianized states (4th century CE). Sacked around 960 CE by the forces associated with the queen Gudit / Yodit, and again in 1535 during the Ethiopian-Adal War by Imam Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi ('Gragn'), who destroyed churches and monasteries across northern Ethiopia. The Obelisk of Aksum (24 m) was looted by the Italian fascist occupation forces in 1937 under Mussolini's orders, shipped to Rome, and only returned to Ethiopia in 2005 after decades of diplomatic pressure — the reassembly was completed in 2008. Italian state responsibility for this cultural theft was only formally acknowledged in the 1997 bilateral agreement (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Phillipson, D. W. (2012). Foundations of an African Civilisation: Aksum and the Northern Horn, 1000 BC–AD 1300. James Currey.
Munro-Hay, S. C. (1991). Aksum: An African Civilisation of Late Antiquity. Edinburgh University Press.

name_variants.name: ʾAksum
name_variants.context: Amharic form

name_variants.name: Axum
name_variants.context: Hispanized/Lusophone-influenced English form

ethical_notes: Capital of the Zagwe dynasty. The city was renamed 'Lalibela' after King Gebre Mesqel Lalibela (r. c. 1181-1221) who commissioned its eleven rock-hewn churches as a 'New Jerusalem' for Ethiopian Orthodox pilgrims cut off from Jerusalem itself by Ayyubid and Mamluk control of the Holy Land. The churches have been threatened since 2021 by the Tigray War; monastic communities have reported shelling damage. UNESCO has repeatedly expressed concern over protective shelter structures built over some churches.

sources.citation: Phillipson, D. W. (2009). Ancient Churches of Ethiopia: Fourth-Fourteenth Centuries. Yale University Press.
Mercier, J., & Lepage, C. (2012). Lalibela, Wonder of Ethiopia: The Monolithic Churches and Their Treasures. Paul Holberton.

name_variants.name: Lalibela
name_variants.context: Renamed after King Lalibela

ethical_notes: Academic and Islamic scholarly center, site of the Sankore, Djinguereber and Sidi Yahia madrasas. The claim that Timbuktu was a 'myth' until the 19th-century European 'discovery' (René Caillié 1828) erases continuous North African and West African knowledge of the city. The libraries suffered three major episodes of loss: the Moroccan invasion of 1591 led by Judar Pasha (Sa'adi) that looted and dispersed scholarly holdings and deported scholars like Ahmad Baba to Marrakesh; the French colonial period extracted manuscripts into European collections; and in 2012-2013 Ansar Dine and AQIM jihadist occupiers destroyed Sufi shrines and attempted to burn manuscripts — thousands were saved by the heroic clandestine 'manuscript airlift' organized by Abdel Kader Haïdara and Bamako-based librarians (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Hunwick, J. O. (2003). Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire: Al-Sa'di's Ta'rikh al-sudan down to 1613 and Other Contemporary Documents. Brill.
Jeppie, S., & Diagne, S. B. (Eds.). (2008). The Meanings of Timbuktu. HSRC Press.
Hammer, J. (2016). The Bad-Ass Librarians of Timbuktu. Simon & Schuster.

name_variants.name: Timbuktu
name_variants.context: Arabic form used in scholarship and travel accounts

name_variants.name: Tombouctou
name_variants.context: French colonial form

ethical_notes: Djenné-Djeno (Old Djenné) is an Inland Niger Delta city that predates Islamic contact, contradicting colonial-era claims that West African urbanism required Arab/North African stimulus. Excavations by Roderick and Susan McIntosh (1977-1981) reestablished autochthonous African origins. The modern successor Djenné is famous for the Great Mosque (first built 1240, rebuilt 1907 by the French administration — the 'photogenic' rebuild is sometimes presented as 'traditional' while being a colonial reconstruction (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: McIntosh, R. J., & McIntosh, S. K. (1980). Prehistoric Investigations at Jenne, Mali. BAR International Series.
LaViolette, A. (2008). Swahili Cosmopolitanism in Africa and the Indian Ocean World, AD 600-1500. Archaeologies, 4(1), 24-49.

name_variants.name: Djenné
name_variants.context: French colonial form used for the successor town

name_variants.name: Jenne
name_variants.context: English form

ethical_notes: Capital of the Songhai Empire at its peak under Askia Muhammad I (r. 1493-1528). The Tomb of Askia (a pyramidal pisé structure) survives. Gao was sacked during the Moroccan invasion of 1591 (Battle of Tondibi, Judar Pasha), an event triggered in part by Moroccan hunger for Sahelian gold. French colonial administration (1894-1960) actively de-prioritized Gao relative to the Senegal coast, contributing to the city's continued decline. The modern town was partially held by MNLA and Islamist groups in 2012 during the Northern Mali conflict.

sources.citation: Hunwick, J. O. (2003). Timbuktu and the Songhay Empire. Brill.
Insoll, T. (2003). The Archaeology of Islam in Sub-Saharan Africa. Cambridge University Press.

ethical_notes: Capital of the Benin Empire (Edo people; not to be confused with the modern Republic of Benin which took its name from the Bight of Benin). In February 1897 the British Punitive Expedition, as retaliation for the death of Acting Consul James Phillips, sacked and burned Edo — the royal palace, compounds, and the surrounding city. Oba Ovonramwen was deposed and exiled to Calabar. Thousands of Benin Bronzes (actually brass and ivory plaques and sculptures documenting six centuries of Edo history) were looted and sold to cover the cost of the expedition; they were dispersed to the British Museum, Berlin Ethnological Museum, Pitt Rivers, the Metropolitan, and private collections. A partial return began in 2021-2022 (Germany's Humboldt Forum, University of Aberdeen, Jesus College Cambridge) but the majority remain in European and North American holdings. The destruction of Benin City is a paradigmatic case of British imperial cultural erasure (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Igbafe, P. A. (1970). The Fall of Benin: A Reassessment. Journal of African History, 11(3), 385-400.
Hicks, D. (2020). The Brutish Museums: The Benin Bronzes, Colonial Violence and Cultural Restitution. Pluto Press.
Bondarenko, D. M. (2015). The Benin Kingdom (13th-19th Centuries) as a Megacommunity. Social Evolution & History, 14(2), 46-76.

name_variants.name: Benin City
name_variants.context: Portuguese-derived name; Portuguese sailors called the Bight 'Benin' from the local title; English retained the form. Catastrophically reinforced after the 1897 sack when the city was rebuilt under colonial administration

name_variants.name: Ubinu
name_variants.context: Earlier Edo form from which Portuguese 'Benin' derives

ethical_notes: Spiritual and origin center of the Yoruba world, seat of the Ọ̀ọ̀ni. Famous for the naturalistic copper-alloy and terracotta heads produced c. 1100-1450 CE. When Leo Frobenius excavated at Ife in 1910-1912 and published the bronzes, European scholarship initially attributed them to Etruscan, Greek or 'lost Atlantis' sources rather than to the Yoruba themselves — an explicit refusal to accept African authorship of sophisticated figurative art. Frobenius himself removed several bronzes to Germany; others went to the British Museum. African and African-diaspora scholars (Ekpo Eyo, Babatunde Lawal) systematically dismantled this racist attribution from the 1970s onward (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Drewal, H. J., & Schildkrout, E. (2009). Dynasty and Divinity: Ife Art in Ancient Nigeria. Museum for African Art.
Eyo, E. (1977). Two Thousand Years Nigerian Art. Federal Department of Antiquities.
Willett, F. (1967). Ife in the History of West African Sculpture. McGraw-Hill.

name_variants.name: Ife
name_variants.context: Anglicized form common in scholarship

ethical_notes: Founded in 1576 by Paulo Dias de Novais as São Paulo da Assunção de Loanda on territory of the Mbundu / Ndongo kingdom. Along with Benguela, Luanda was the largest embarkation port of the trans-Atlantic slave trade, with an estimated 5.7 million captured Africans shipped through Angolan ports between the 16th and 19th centuries, most bound for Brazil. Queen Njinga (Nzinga Mbande, r. 1624-1663) waged sustained resistance against Portuguese expansion. After the 1961 Baixa de Cassanje massacre and the MPLA/UPA/FNLA anti-colonial war, Portugal only withdrew in 1975 — leaving a city shaped by four centuries of racialized labor extraction (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Heywood, L. M. (2017). Njinga of Angola: Africa's Warrior Queen. Harvard University Press.
Miller, J. C. (1988). Way of Death: Merchant Capitalism and the Angolan Slave Trade, 1730-1830. University of Wisconsin Press.
Thornton, J. K. (1998). Africa and Africans in the Making of the Atlantic World, 1400-1800. Cambridge University Press.

name_variants.name: São Paulo da Assunção de Loanda
name_variants.context: Portuguese colonial full name

name_variants.name: Loanda
name_variants.context: Older Portuguese spelling

ethical_notes: Capital of the Kingdom of Loango on the Atlantic coast north of the Kongo kingdom. The adjacent port Loango Bay was among the principal embarkation points of the trans-Atlantic slave trade in Central Africa from the 17th through early 19th centuries, with major Dutch, French and Portuguese traffic — contributing several hundred thousand enslaved people to the Atlantic trade. Absorbed into French Equatorial Africa in 1883; the former capital was largely abandoned in favor of Loango and later Pointe-Noire. Loango ceremonial objects are dispersed across European ethnographic collections without systematic restitution (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Martin, P. M. (1972). The External Trade of the Loango Coast, 1576-1870. Clarendon Press.
Heywood, L. M., & Thornton, J. K. (2007). Central Africans, Atlantic Creoles, and the Foundation of the Americas, 1585-1660. Cambridge University Press.

name_variants.name: Loango
name_variants.context: Portuguese form also used in English

name_variants.name: Buali
name_variants.context: Alternate indigenous name for the capital

ethical_notes: Capital of the Novgorod Republic (1136-1478), one of medieval Europe's most democratic polities via its veche assembly. Forcibly annexed to Muscovy by Ivan III in 1478 (veche bell sent to Moscow as symbol of subjugation); sacked again by Ivan IV the Terrible in the Massacre of 1570 with estimated 10,000-60,000 killed — one of the worst state-directed massacres in European medieval history. The massacre was deliberately framed by Muscovite propaganda as necessary for unification; modern Russian historiography still struggles to confront it honestly. Devastated again during the Nazi occupation 1941-1944.

sources.citation: Michels, G. B. (2018). The Romanovs and Novgorod: 300 Years of History. Routledge.
Halperin, C. J. (1985). Russia and the Golden Horde. Indiana University Press.
Birnbaum, H. (1996). Novgorod in Focus: Selected Essays. Slavica Publishers.

name_variants.name: Великий Новгород
name_variants.context: Official modern Russian name 'Veliky Novgorod'

name_variants.name: Holmgarðr
name_variants.context: Old Norse name used in Viking Age sources

ethical_notes: Capital of Kyivan Rus'. Sacked by the Mongols under Batu Khan in December 1240 after a brutal siege; according to Plano Carpini who passed through six years later, 'scarcely 200 houses' remained standing. The city never fully recovered its medieval primacy. Under the Russian Empire and Soviet Union, Ukrainian language and culture were systematically suppressed (Valuev Circular 1863, Ems Ukaz 1876, Soviet Russification). The 1932-1933 Holodomor famine killed millions of Ukrainians and is recognized by many states as genocide. The English spelling 'Kiev' transliterates Russian 'Киев'; the modern Ukrainian 'Kyiv' transliterates 'Київ' and its international adoption (since 2019 by most English-language media and governments) is an explicit act of linguistic decolonization. Further devastated by the Russian invasion beginning in 2022 (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Plokhy, S. (2015). The Gates of Europe: A History of Ukraine. Basic Books.
Halperin, C. J. (1985). Russia and the Golden Horde. Indiana University Press.
Magocsi, P. R. (2010). A History of Ukraine: The Land and Its Peoples (2nd ed.). University of Toronto Press.

name_variants.name: Київ
name_variants.context: Modern Ukrainian form; 'Kyiv' in international transliteration, reclaimed in global usage from 2019 replacing the Russian-derived 'Kiev'

name_variants.name: Киев
name_variants.context: Russian form, source of the Russian-derived English 'Kiev' used through the 20th century

name_variants.name: Kiev
name_variants.context: English form derived from Russian transliteration, widely replaced from 2019 by 'Kyiv'

ethical_notes: Swedish fortress and town on the Neva delta in Ingria (Ingermanland), the region depopulated of its Finnic Ingrian inhabitants after decades of Swedish-Russian warfare. Captured by Peter the Great on May 1, 1703 during the Great Northern War; within days, on May 27, 1703, Peter founded Saint Petersburg on essentially the same site. Nyenskans was demolished; its population was displaced. The founding narrative of Saint Petersburg as built 'on a swamp' by Peter the Great erases the pre-existing Swedish town and the even older Ingrian villages. The Russian spelling 'Ниеншанц' reflects the Swedish original.

sources.citation: Kirby, D. (1990). Northern Europe in the Early Modern Period: The Baltic World 1492-1772. Longman.
Bassin, M. (1991). Russia Between Europe and Asia. Slavic Review, 50(1), 1-17.

name_variants.name: Ниеншанц
name_variants.context: Russian transliteration used in historiography

name_variants.name: Kantsi
name_variants.context: Finnish name

ethical_notes: Major Viking-age trade hub on Björkö island in Lake Mälaren. Abandoned around 975; the political and commercial center of the Mälaren region shifted to nearby Sigtuna. Archaeology (particularly the 1873-1895 Stolpe excavations) uncovered grave goods linking Birka to the Byzantine Empire, the Abbasid Caliphate (Islamic silver dirhams), Frankish lands and Finno-Ugric peoples. The 2017 reinterpretation of the Bj 581 grave as a female warrior challenged long-standing male-default assumptions in Viking archaeology.

sources.citation: Ambrosiani, B. (2013). Excavations in the Black Earth 1990-1995. Stockholm University.
Hedenstierna-Jonson, C., et al. (2017). A female Viking warrior confirmed by genomics. American Journal of Physical Anthropology, 164(4), 853-860.

ethical_notes: Old Norse 'Heiðabýr' ('heath-settlement'), attested in runic and skaldic sources. One of the largest Viking-age towns, straddling Danish-Saxon frontier. Destroyed in 1066 by Harald Hardrada's Norwegians and never rebuilt; its role passed to Schleswig on the opposite shore. The German name 'Haithabu' is a modern scholarly reconstruction; Danish 'Hedeby' became standard in Scandinavian literature. The 19th-century Schleswig-Holstein Question used the site symbolically to bolster competing Danish and German territorial claims — archaeology here has not been ideologically neutral.

sources.citation: Hilberg, V. (2009). Hedeby in Wulfstan's Days: A Danish Emporium of the Viking Age Between West and East. In Wulfstan's Voyage. Viking Ship Museum.
Hodges, R. (2012). Dark Age Economics: A New Audit. Bristol Classical Press.

name_variants.name: Hedeby
name_variants.context: Modern Danish form

name_variants.name: Haithabu
name_variants.context: Reconstructed German form standard in German-language scholarship

ethical_notes: Gamla Uppsala ('Old Uppsala'), royal seat and principal pre-Christian cult site of the Svear, attested by Adam of Bremen (c. 1070) who described the 'Temple of Uppsala' with sacrifices of men, horses and dogs every nine years. Adam's account is externally written (a Christianizing Hamburg-Bremen clerical source) and may exaggerate; there is no indigenous written source from pre-Christian Svear. The Christianization process destroyed the temple buildings (likely late 11th century); the modern cathedral site at (new) Uppsala was deliberately chosen to eclipse the pagan center — an early medieval example of the pattern of superimposition seen elsewhere in the world.

sources.citation: Hultgård, A. (1997). From the Old Norse Religion to Christianity. In The Christianization of Scandinavia. Viktoria Bokförlag.
Sundqvist, O. (2002). Freyr's Offspring: Rulers and Religion in Ancient Svea Society. Uppsala University.

name_variants.name: Gamla Uppsala
name_variants.context: Modern Swedish 'Old Uppsala' distinguishing it from the later episcopal town

ethical_notes: Hanseatic trade hub on Gotland. Sacked by the Danish King Valdemar IV Atterdag in 1361 — the Battle of Visby left approximately 1800 defenders (many farmers, elderly, and boys) dead outside the walls, their bodies buried in mass graves later excavated 1905-1928 (Bengt Thordeman's work is a milestone in skeletal archaeology of battle). Valdemar reportedly ransomed the town for its treasure under threat of burning it; the so-called 'Atterdag tower' legend memorializes this extortion. The city then declined relative to Lübeck's rising Hansa dominance.

sources.citation: Thordeman, B. (1939). Armour from the Battle of Wisby 1361. Kungl. Vitterhets Historie och Antikvitets Akademien.
Yrwing, H. (1986). Visby: Hansestad på Gotland. Gidlund.

ethical_notes: Founded 1201 by Bishop Albert of Riga as a forward base for the Northern Crusades against the Baltic Finnic and Baltic-speaking pagans (Livs, Latgalians, Selonians). The indigenous Liv people were progressively converted, dispossessed and ethnically reduced — the Livonian language went extinct with its last native speaker Grizelda Kristiņa in 2013. Under the Teutonic Order, Hansa, Swedish, Polish, Russian Empire, independent Latvia, Nazi occupation (Holocaust: the Rumbula and Biķernieki massacres killed ~25,000 Latvian Jews and 25,000 deported German Jews), Soviet occupation (mass deportations of Latvians to Siberia 1941 and 1949), and re-independence in 1991. The 20th-century Russification and the post-1991 language policies are part of ongoing ethnolinguistic debates.

sources.citation: Plakans, A. (2011). A Concise History of the Baltic States. Cambridge University Press.
Urban, W. (2003). The Teutonic Knights: A Military History. Greenhill Books.

name_variants.name: Riga
name_variants.context: German form used during Baltic German and Hanseatic periods

name_variants.name: Рига
name_variants.context: Russian form used during Russian Empire and Soviet Union

ethical_notes: The original settlement 'Liubice' was a Polabian Slavic (Obodrite) town attested from the 9th century. In 1138 it was destroyed during the Wendish wars; Henry the Lion refounded 'Lübeck' on a new site in 1143 as a German town. The Ostsiedlung and Wendish Crusade (1147) systematically displaced, Christianized or assimilated Polabian Slavs; the Polabian language disappeared by c. 1750 (last speakers in the Wendland, Lower Saxony). The founding narrative of Lübeck as 'Königin der Hanse' (Queen of the Hansa) celebrates the German-Hanseatic city while rarely acknowledging the displaced Slavic substrate. This pattern — German Drang nach Osten as a form of colonization within Europe — was central to Nazi ideology and remains a sensitive theme in German historiography (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Dollinger, P. (1970). The German Hansa. Macmillan.
Christiansen, E. (1997). The Northern Crusades (2nd ed.). Penguin.
Lotter, F. (1989). The Crusading Idea and the Conquest of the Region East of the Elbe. In Medieval Frontier Societies (pp. 267-306). Clarendon Press.

name_variants.name: Lübeck
name_variants.context: German refounded town after the destruction of Slavic Liubice

name_variants.name: Lubeka
name_variants.context: Polish form

ethical_notes: Capital of medieval Norway; seat of the Bergenhus royal residence and of the Hanseatic Kontor Tyskebryggen from c. 1360 to 1754. The Bryggen wooden tenements preserve the Hansa commercial landscape. Under the Kalmar Union and then Danish rule (1536-1814), Norwegian culture was subordinated to Copenhagen; the 19th-century language debate (Bokmål vs. Nynorsk) reflected the ongoing struggle against Danicization. Damaged by Nazi German occupation and the 1944 D/S Voorbode explosion (ammunition ship disaster). The Hansa presence was simultaneously economically dominant and demographically segregated — German merchants in Bergen were legally separated from the Norwegian population, prefiguring later European patterns.

sources.citation: Helle, K. (1982). Bergen bys historie. Universitetsforlaget.
Jahnke, C. (2013). Die Hanse. Reclam.

name_variants.name: Bergen
name_variants.context: Modern Norwegian form

name_variants.name: Bergen
name_variants.context: German Hansa usage

ethical_notes: The first attested form of the name is the Slavic 'Gyddanyzc' (997) and Polish 'Gdańsk'; the German 'Danzig' derives from this. The city was a Slavic Pomeranian foundation, then under Teutonic Order rule 1308-1466 (after the massacre of Gdańsk in 1308 when the Teutonic Knights seized the city and reportedly killed many inhabitants), then part of the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth as a self-governing city with substantial German-speaking bourgeoisie. After the Second Partition (1793) it was annexed by Prussia. The Free City of Danzig (1920-1939) under League of Nations mandate had a German majority population — this pretext was used by Hitler to launch the invasion of Poland on September 1, 1939 at Westerplatte. After 1945 the surviving German population was expelled under the Potsdam Agreement (approximately 100,000-125,000 expelled); the city was Polonized as Gdańsk with Poles displaced from eastern territories lost to the USSR resettled there. The historic old town (destroyed by Soviet and German fighting in 1945, 90% in ruins) was meticulously rebuilt as 'Polish' heritage, a reconstruction that emphasizes Polish continuity and de-emphasizes the centuries of German-Polish bi-cultural history (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Biskup, M. (1990). Historia Gdańska, t. I-IV. Wydawnictwo Morskie.
Loew, P. O. (2011). Danzig. Biographie einer Stadt. C.H. Beck.
Tighe, C. (1990). Gdańsk: National Identity in the Polish-German Borderlands. Pluto Press.

name_variants.name: Danzig
name_variants.context: German form dominant during Teutonic and Prussian periods, and during the Free City 1920-1939 and Nazi annexation 1939-1945

name_variants.name: Gyddanyzc
name_variants.context: Earliest attested Slavic form in the Life of Saint Adalbert

ethical_notes: Originally a Silesian Slavic settlement, name derived from Duke Wrocisław. Under the Piast dynasty, then Bohemian Crown from 1335, Habsburgs from 1526, Prussian Kingdom from 1741 as Breslau, and part of Germany until 1945. Breslau had a substantial German-speaking majority by the modern period and a significant Jewish community (exterminated in the Holocaust). During 1945 the city was declared 'Festung Breslau' by the Nazis and devastated in the 3-month siege (February-May 1945), with approximately 70% of the historic center destroyed. The surviving German population was expelled under the Potsdam Agreement (1945-1948, approximately 350,000 expelled). The city was Polonized as Wrocław and repopulated with Poles displaced from Lwów and other eastern territories lost to the USSR — a carefully orchestrated demographic and cultural substitution sometimes called 'Operation Vistula' in its broader pattern. Wrocław's historic center was meticulously rebuilt; the 2016 European Capital of Culture year officially acknowledged the multicultural (German, Jewish, Czech, Polish) heritage that Communist-era histories had downplayed (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Davies, N., & Moorhouse, R. (2002). Microcosm: Portrait of a Central European City. Jonathan Cape.
Thum, G. (2011). Uprooted: How Breslau Became Wrocław during the Century of Expulsions. Princeton University Press.

name_variants.name: Breslau
name_variants.context: German form dominant under Bohemian, Habsburg, Prussian and Nazi rule

name_variants.name: Vratislav
name_variants.context: Czech form during Bohemian Crown period

ethical_notes: Originally Twangste, an Old Prussian (Baltic) settlement destroyed in 1255 by the Teutonic Order during the Prussian Crusade. The Teutonic Knights founded a new castle 'Königsberg' (King's Mountain, after King Ottokar II of Bohemia) the same year; Old Prussian language and culture were systematically exterminated during the 13th-16th centuries (last native speakers late 17th century) — one of the most complete ethnic and linguistic erasures in European history. Königsberg became the capital of the Duchy of Prussia (1525), then Brandenburg-Prussia, then German Empire. Home of Immanuel Kant (buried 1804 in the cathedral). In 1945 the city was devastated by British Bomber Command raids (August 1944) and the Soviet Königsberg Offensive (April 1945). The entire remaining German population (several hundred thousand) was expelled 1945-1948 under the Potsdam Agreement — many died during expulsion. The city was renamed Kaliningrad on July 4, 1946, after Soviet politician Mikhail Kalinin (who had in fact died days earlier and was complicit in Stalinist crimes including the Katyn massacre order). It was repopulated with Russians and other Soviet citizens and became a closed military zone. Kant's grave is among the very few pre-1945 monuments preserved; most historic buildings were deliberately demolished by Soviet authorities in the 1950s-60s (Königsberg Castle ruins dynamited in 1968 on Brezhnev's order). Kaliningrad remains a Russian exclave whose historical identity is actively contested (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Kossert, A. (2008). Damals in Ostpreußen. Der Untergang einer deutschen Provinz. Siedler.
Clark, C. (2006). Iron Kingdom: The Rise and Downfall of Prussia, 1600-1947. Harvard University Press.
Sahm, U. (2021). Kaliningrad. Ein Requiem auf Ostpreußen. Langen Müller.

name_variants.name: Königsberg
name_variants.context: German name from the Teutonic Order foundation until the Soviet annexation in 1945. The entire German population was expelled 1945-1948

name_variants.name: Калининград
name_variants.context: Soviet rename on July 4, 1946 after Mikhail Kalinin. The city was repopulated with Russians after the expulsion of Germans; Königsberg Castle was dynamited 1968

name_variants.name: Królewiec
name_variants.context: Polish form used during Polish suzerainty 1466-1657 and in modern Polish

name_variants.name: Karaliaučius
name_variants.context: Lithuanian form; the region was a center of Lithuanian-language publishing during Prussian rule

ethical_notes: Founded by the Rus' prince Daniel of Galicia-Volhynia and named for his son Lev. The city's demographic history is radically layered: Ruthenian/Ukrainian, Polish, Armenian, Jewish, and German populations coexisted for centuries. In 1939 Lwów was about 50% Polish, 30% Jewish, 16% Ukrainian. The Holocaust exterminated the Jewish population (approximately 140,000 killed, the Janowska concentration camp was in the city). In 1944-1946 Polish residents were forcibly transferred to post-war Poland under the Soviet-Polish 'population exchange' (approximately 100,000-150,000 Poles expelled); in parallel, Ukrainians were expelled from post-war Polish territories in 'Operation Vistula' (1947). The city was Sovietized and became majority Ukrainian only after these population transfers — the Polish character was not 'lost naturally' but extirpated by state policy. The Ukrainian name 'Львів' (Lviv) is the correct modern transliteration; 'Lwów' is Polish, 'Lemberg' is Austrian German (city was under Habsburg rule 1772-1918, capital of Kingdom of Galicia and Lodomeria). All three names describe distinct historical periods — erasing any of them falsifies the record (ETHICS-009).

sources.citation: Prusin, A. V. (2010). The Lands Between: Conflict in the East European Borderlands, 1870-1992. Oxford University Press.
Mick, C. (2016). Lemberg, Lwów, L'viv, 1914-1947: Violence and Ethnicity in a Contested City. Purdue University Press.
Snyder, T. (2003). The Reconstruction of Nations: Poland, Ukraine, Lithuania, Belarus, 1569-1999. Yale University Press.

name_variants.name: Lwów
name_variants.context: Polish form during Polish Crown (1340-1772) and interwar Polish rule (1918-1939); the Polish population was expelled 1944-1946

name_variants.name: Lemberg
name_variants.context: Austrian German form used under Habsburg rule as capital of Galicia and Lodomeria

name_variants.name: לעמבערג
name_variants.context: Yiddish form used by the large Jewish community exterminated in the Holocaust

name_variants.name: Львов
name_variants.context: Russian form used during Soviet rule
